strategic_overview: The statement is very likely true for this particular SPDE, but not for a purely formal reason from the assumptions as written. Full additive space-time white noise should destroy invariant components, while the Dirac drift is a gradient drift of a bounded jump potential, so there is a natural Gibbs candidate equivalent to Brownian bridge law. The main difficulty is upgrading the ABLM-style weak mild solution and approximation convergence into one of the standard uniqueness mechanisms: transition-kernel equivalence, strong/asymptotic strong Feller plus irreducibility, or a Dirichlet-form/Gibbs characterization.

directions.name: Gibbs Dirichlet-form route
directions.description: Treat δ0 as the distributional derivative of the bounded potential f(r) = -2 1_{r >= 0}, so that -0.5 f' = δ0. The candidate invariant law is ν(du) proportional to exp(2 ∫_0^1 1_{u(x) >= 0} dx) times Brownian bridge law. Prove that the ABLM Dirichlet solution is the same Markov process as the symmetric Dirichlet-form process with this ν. Then show any invariant probability μ is absolutely continuous with respect to ν; by symmetry and irreducibility of the gradient form, μ must equal ν.
key_ideas: Use the bounded-density equivalence ν ~ Brownian bridge to transfer irreducibility of the Gaussian gradient form.
If R_λ(x,dy) is absolutely continuous with respect to ν for every starting point x, then every invariant μ satisfies μ << ν because μ R_λ = μ / λ.
Once μ << ν, write h = dμ/dν. Invariance and ν-symmetry give P_t h = h in L2(ν); irreducibility forces h constant.
relevant_techniques: Paper 3, A Skew Stochastic Heat Equation, https://arxiv.org/abs/1105.2779: Gibbs measure construction, Lemma 2.4 closability, Proposition 3.1 invariance of the symmetric process, Proposition 3.4 integration by parts with Brownian bridge local times, Proposition 2.5 resolvent absolute continuity, and Propositions 4.7-4.8 Mosco convergence of smooth approximations.

Paper 6, Well-posedness of stochastic heat equation with distributional drift and skew stochastic heat equation, https://arxiv.org/abs/2011.13498: Theorem 2.10 approximation/stability and Theorem 2.8 for finite non-negative measure drifts; use this to identify mollified δ0 equations with the limiting ABLM solution.

Paper 12, Analytically weak and mild solutions to stochastic heat equation with irregular drift, https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40072-025-00392-x: Theorem 2.10 and Lemma 4.1 justify compatibility of weak, mild, and regularized drift formulations.
directions.potential_obstacles: The decisive gap is removing exceptional sets. Paper 3 gives resolvent absolute continuity only outside a capacity-zero exceptional set in the Dirichlet-form construction, not automatically for every deterministic initial condition in C_0([0,1]). One must also prove that the ABLM Markov solution coincides with the Dirichlet-form process for all initial data, not merely ν-a.e. The sign convention for f should be checked carefully, but this is minor.

directions.name: Strong Feller plus irreducibility
directions.description: Prove directly that P_t is strong Feller on C_0([0,1]) and topologically irreducible. Then all positive-time transition probabilities are mutually equivalent, and any invariant probability is unique. For the smooth equations with drift G_{1/n}, this is standard; the real work is obtaining estimates stable as n tends to infinity or proving the properties directly for the singular equation.
key_ideas: For each smooth approximant, use additive nondegenerate noise and parabolic smoothing to get strong Feller and support/irreducibility.
Try to pass strong Feller through uniform Bismut or Kolmogorov estimates expressed in Besov norms rather than in sup norms of G_{1/n}.
Prove irreducibility by a support theorem: controlled additive noise should be able to track smooth paths ending in any prescribed C_0-neighborhood.
relevant_techniques: Paper 8, Invariant measures for nonlinear SPDE's: uniqueness and stability, https://dml.cz/handle/10338.dmlcz/107641: Theorem 4 and Lemma 6 give uniqueness from mutually equivalent kernels; Theorem 8 and Example 3 give the linear Dirichlet heat nondegeneracy check.

Paper 9, Ergodic behaviour of stochastic parabolic equations, https://dml.cz/handle/10338.dmlcz/127357: Proposition 2.5 says finite invariant measure plus equivalent positive-time kernels implies total-variation coalescence; Examples 3.3, 3.5, and 3.6 show the classical Dirichlet reaction-diffusion template.

Paper 4, Small Ball Probabilities and a Support Theorem for the Stochastic Heat Equation, https://arxiv.org/abs/2006.07978, and Paper 7, A Support Theorem for Parabolic Stochastic PDEs with Nondegenerate Hölder Diffusion Coefficients, https://arxiv.org/abs/2302.00502: use their support/small-ball strategy for additive noise, adapted to Dirichlet heat kernels.

Paper 20, Uniqueness of the Invariant Measure for a Stochastic PDE Driven by Degenerate Noise, https://www.hairer.org/papers/unique.pdf: Theorem 4.1 and the cutoff-localization technique are a model for proving strong Feller first for regularized/cutoff dynamics and then removing the cutoff.
directions.potential_obstacles: Weak convergence u^n(t) => u(t) is not enough to pass open-set lower bounds or strong Feller continuity. The approximating drifts have ||G_{1/n}||_∞ and ||G_{1/n}'||_∞ blowing up, so standard bounded-drift or Lipschitz-drift strong Feller constants are useless unless replaced by estimates depending on the negative Besov norm of δ0.

directions.name: Asymptotic strong Feller plus common support point
directions.description: Instead of proving full strong Feller, prove an asymptotic gradient estimate and then show that every invariant measure has a common support point, preferably 0 or the Gibbs support. This is often easier in infinite dimensions because high Dirichlet modes are damped by the heat semigroup and only finitely many low modes need probabilistic smoothing.
key_ideas: Aim for a bound of the form ||D P_{t_n} φ(u)|| <= C(u)(||φ||_∞ + δ_n ||Dφ||_∞) with δ_n -> 0.
Use full space-time white noise to smooth low modes and heat dissipation to kill high modes.
Show 0 lies in the support of every invariant measure using a moment/tightness bound plus positive-probability accessibility of small balls around 0.
relevant_techniques: Paper 14, Ergodicity of the 2D Navier-Stokes equations with degenerate stochastic forcing, https://arxiv.org/abs/math/0406087: Proposition 3.12 gradient criterion, Theorem 3.16 support separation, Corollary 3.17 common-support uniqueness, and Corollary 4.2 for the common-support-point argument.

Paper 1, A Theory of Hypoellipticity and Unique Ergodicity for Semilinear Stochastic PDEs, https://arxiv.org/abs/0808.1361: Proposition 1.1 and Corollary 1.4 give the same asymptotic strong Feller uniqueness architecture; Theorem 5.5 shows how to transfer initial perturbations to noise with a residual.
Paper 4, https://arxiv.org/abs/2006.07978: small-ball and support estimates are the natural way to prove accessibility of a neighborhood of 0 from bounded sets.
directions.potential_obstacles: The common-support step needs a uniform bound for arbitrary invariant measures, not just for the Gibbs candidate. The smoothing step again requires estimates for the singular δ0 drift that are uniform under mollification. Also, reaching a small ball around 0 is delicate because the δ0 drift is concentrated exactly at zero and pushes in a biased direction.

directions.name: Stationary Fokker-Planck or logarithmic-derivative characterization
directions.description: Assume μ is invariant and derive the stationary integration-by-parts identity for cylinder functions. Show that this identity uniquely characterizes the Gibbs law ν. This would prove uniqueness without first proving strong Feller or pointwise kernel equivalence.
key_ideas: For smooth approximants, invariant measures solve an explicit stationary Fokker-Planck equation with density exp(-∫ f_n(u(x)) dx) relative to Brownian bridge law.
Pass to the δ0 limit and identify the logarithmic derivative as the Brownian bridge Gaussian derivative plus the local-time term at level 0.
Use uniqueness of the measure with this logarithmic derivative, or equivalently irreducibility of the associated gradient Dirichlet form.
relevant_techniques: Paper 3, https://arxiv.org/abs/1105.2779: Proposition 3.4 gives the exact Brownian-bridge integration-by-parts formula with local times; Proposition 3.5 identifies the Fukushima drift term; the bounded-density transfer in Lemma 2.4 is crucial.
Paper 6, https://arxiv.org/abs/2011.13498: Definition 2.3 and Theorem 2.10 explain how the distributional drift is recovered from every smooth approximation.
Paper 12, https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40072-025-00392-x: Lemma 4.1 and Corollary 3.5 give approximation tools for drift integrals in negative regularity.
directions.potential_obstacles: For an arbitrary invariant μ, it is nontrivial to justify the generator identity involving δ0(u). One needs μ-integrability of the regularized drift terms or occupation/local-time bounds under μ. This approach may collapse back to proving μ << ν, so it is best viewed as a complement to the Dirichlet-form route.

directions.name: High-risk log-Harnack coupling
directions.description: Try to adapt Wang's coupling-by-change-of-measure method to the δ0 drift. If successful, a log-Harnack inequality would imply strong Feller-type regularity and uniqueness of invariant measures almost immediately.
key_ideas: Couple two solutions by adding a terminal singular feedback γ_t^{-1}(X_t - Y_t) to one equation.
Absorb the feedback into the additive white noise by Girsanov.
Prove an energy inequality forcing X_T = Y_T under the changed measure.
relevant_techniques: Paper 5, Harnack Inequalities and Ergodicity of Stochastic Reaction-Diffusion Equation in Lp, https://arxiv.org/abs/2008.01335: Lemmas 3.2-3.4 and Theorem 4.1 give the exact coupling, entropy, power-Harnack, and uniqueness mechanism.
Paper 6, https://arxiv.org/abs/2011.13498: Corollary 2.11 comparison and Proposition 3.8 for positive measure drifts may help formulate the drift contribution in an energy estimate.
directions.potential_obstacles: This is elegant but probably not the first route to try. The smooth drifts G_{1/n} do not have a uniform one-sided Lipschitz constant, and it is unclear whether the δ0 local-time drift is dissipative enough for the required difference estimate. Without a new local-time monotonicity identity, the Harnack constants will blow up.

key_obstacles: The main barriers are: proving any smoothing property for the limiting singular semigroup rather than for fixed smooth approximants; removing Dirichlet-form exceptional sets so statements hold for every initial condition in C_0([0,1]); proving absolute continuity of transition or resolvent kernels with respect to the Gibbs/Brownian-bridge reference law; and obtaining invariant-measure moment or tightness bounds for arbitrary invariant measures. A recurring warning is that weak convergence of approximating solutions at fixed deterministic initial data does not by itself preserve irreducibility, lower bounds, strong Feller estimates, or uniqueness of invariant measures.
recommended_starting_point: Start with the Gibbs Dirichlet-form route. First write down f_n(r) = -2 ∫_{-∞}^r G_{1/n}(a) da, so that -0.5 f_n'(r) = G_{1/n}(r), and verify that the smooth approximating SPDEs are reversible with ν_n proportional to exp(-∫ f_n(u(x)) dx) times Brownian bridge law. Then prove ν_n => ν with ν proportional to exp(2 ∫ 1_{u(x) >= 0} dx) times Brownian bridge law. The first serious target should be: prove R_λ(x,dy) << ν for every x in C_0([0,1]). If that is achieved, uniqueness follows by the short irreducible symmetric-form argument.
